title: CodeCombat
description: Medieval-style online game where you control characters by typing real Python or JavaScript code. One of the best transitions to text coding for grades 5 and up.

title: Learn Code with Python — boot.dev
description: Interactive, game-like Python course that teaches coding through hands-on challenges and projects.

title: Codedex
description: Fun and beginner-friendly platform to learn how to code through gamified lessons and quests.

title: Coding for Kids — Python Courses
description: Kid-friendly Python courses designed to introduce programming concepts through guided exercises.

title: Flexbox Froggy
description: Interactive puzzle game that teaches CSS Flexbox by guiding frogs to lily pads.

title: CSS Grid Garden
description: Learn CSS Grid Layout fundamentals through fun, visual gardening challenges.

title: Learn Git Branching
description: Interactive visual game that teaches Git branching, merging, rebasing, and version control concepts.

title: Minecraft Adventurer — Code.org
description: Teaches core CS concepts — commands, repeat loops, and if statements — through a Minecraft-themed coding workspace.

title: Scratch — Imagine, Program, Share
description: Free drag-and-drop block coding from MIT. Students build games, animations, and interactive stories. Core tool for creative coding modules.

title: Blockly Games
description: Collection of puzzle-style coding games by Google that teach programming logic step by step — maze, bird, turtle, and more.

title: Code.org Sprite Lab
description: Students create their own games and animations using drag-and-drop coding blocks. Great for grades 3–5.

title: Code.org Hour of Code
description: Self-paced coding activities from beginner to intermediate, including Minecraft, Star Wars, and Frozen-themed projects. Great for any session opener.

title: Lightbot
description: Puzzle game teaching sequencing, loops, and procedures with no syntax required. Lightbot Jr. is ideal for grades 3–4.

title: Code.org CS Fundamentals
description: Full structured curriculum for grades 3–8 covering sequences, loops, events, conditionals, and functions. Each lesson includes an unplugged warm-up and a coding activity.

title: Code Club World
description: MIT-backed platform with guided projects in Scratch, Python, and HTML. Students follow structured paths and build real projects at their own pace.

title: CodeMonkey
description: Students write real CoffeeScript and Python commands to guide a monkey through levels. Bridges the gap between block coding and text coding.

title: Gamestar Mechanic
description: Students learn game design by building and playing their own video games. Covers design thinking, logic, and systems in a game-based environment.

title: Code for Life — Rapid Router
description: Non-profit platform where students use Blockly to guide a delivery van through routes, then transition to Python. Includes full lesson plans for teachers.

title: CS Unplugged
description: Classroom activities teaching CS concepts through games and puzzles — binary numbers, algorithms, sorting, encryption. No devices required.

title: Code.org Unplugged Lessons
description: Printable lesson plans for teaching loops, conditionals, functions, and debugging through physical activities. Includes the classic Exact Instructions Challenge.

title: Bebras Challenge
description: International computational thinking competition for grades 3–12 with printable problem sets. Great as warm-up challenges or session openers. No coding required.

title: Hello Ruby
description: Storybook character that teaches computational thinking through activities, puzzles, and adventures. Specifically designed for younger learners in grades 3–5.

title: Common Sense Media — Digital Citizenship
description: Lesson plans and activities teaching digital citizenship, online safety, and how the internet works. Useful as a session topic when devices or materials aren't available.

title: Programming Games with Solar 2D
description: YouTube series showing how to build games using the Solar 2D game engine and Lua scripting.

title: Programming Games with Lua
description: Video guide to building games using Lua, the scripting language behind Solar 2D and Roblox.

title: Scratch Team — YouTube
description: Official tutorials, tips, and project walkthroughs from the MIT Scratch team. Best starting point for creative coding project ideas.

title: Griffpatch — YouTube
description: Builds real games in Scratch — platformers, scrollers, and physics engines. Great for students who want to go further after the basics.

title: Google CS First
description: Free videos and curriculum covering storytelling, game design, and creative coding. Short episode format with friendly, diverse presenters.

title: Crash Course Computer Science
description: 40-episode series explaining how computers and CS work — binary, logic gates, algorithms, and the internet. Best for grades 6–8.

title: Micro:bit Foundation — YouTube
description: Official tutorial videos for every Micro:bit project, from the LED display to radio communication and sensors.

title: CS Dojo — YouTube
description: Beginner-friendly Python tutorials in short, clear videos. One of the best starting points for grades 6–8 students learning Python independently.

title: STEMpedia — YouTube
description: Tutorials covering Scratch projects, physical computing, and robotics using common materials. Connects directly to hardware modules.

title: freeCodeCamp — YouTube
description: Long-form beginner tutorials on Python, web development, and CS concepts. Better suited for volunteers looking to build their own background knowledge.

title: Coding with Kids — YouTube
description: Short, friendly coding videos made specifically for elementary and middle school students. Good for students to watch independently between sessions.

title: SQLBolt — Learn SQL
description: Free interactive introduction to SQL — covers SELECT, filtering, joins, and more through hands-on exercises.

title: The Odin Project — Foundations
description: Hands-on introduction to all the essential tools needed to build real, working websites — HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Git.

title: The Odin Project — Full Stack JavaScript
description: In-depth introduction to JavaScript and full-stack web development, building on the Foundations path.

title: GeeksForGeeks
description: Comprehensive library of tutorials, coding problems, and explanations across all major computer science topics.

title: Android Developers — Get Started
description: Official Android development guides and video tutorials for building your first Android app.

title: Khan Academy — Computing
description: Video lessons and interactive exercises covering programming fundamentals, JavaScript, and HTML/CSS. Structured and self-paced with instant feedback.

title: CS Field Guide
description: Interactive online textbook covering algorithms, data, programming languages, and AI with built-in activities. Best for grades 7–8 or volunteer prep.

title: Visual Studio Code
description: Free, lightweight code editor from Microsoft — the most popular editor for web and Python development.

title: Git — Windows Installer
description: Official Git installer for Windows. Required for version control and working with GitHub repositories.

title: Android Studio
description: Official IDE for Android development. Includes an emulator, debugger, and Kotlin/Java toolchain.

title: Solar2D — 2D Game Engine
description: Free, open-source 2D game engine using the Lua scripting language. Great for building mobile games.

title: Microsoft MakeCode
description: Free block and text coding environment used for Micro:bit and robotics. Includes free guided lesson plans at makecode.com/teach.

title: Minecraft Education Edition
description: Free lesson library (license required for the game). Hundreds of educator-made lessons including Python coding. Directly relevant to robotics and coding modules.

title: Replit
description: Browser-based code editor where students can write and run real Python, JavaScript, and more with nothing to install. Great for older students in grades 6–8.

title: Swift Playgrounds
description: Apple's iPad and Mac app that teaches coding through interactive puzzles using Swift. Highly visual, self-paced, and well-designed for grades 5–8.

title: Trinket
description: Browser-based Python and block coding environment with no setup. Great for practicing Python or writing and sharing code between sessions.

title: Google Teachable Machine
description: Train a simple AI model using your webcam or microphone in minutes. A great one-session introduction to how machine learning works for grades 5–8.

title: Typing.com
description: Teaches touch typing through games and lessons. Highly practical for grades 3–5 who struggle to type fast enough to keep up with text coding.
